data.title: Grid reliability projected to decline as data centers drive demand, watchdog says
data.description:
The outlook for electric grid reliability in North America is worsening, as demand may outpace supply in the years ahead, according to a key watchdog.

In its annual Long-Term Reliability Assessment, the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC) warned that demand from new data centers and other large loads is expected to outpace energy supply growth.

The international regulatory authority significantly ratcheted up its 10-year summer peak demand forecast, expecting demand growth to be 69 percent higher than what it projected in last year’s report. 

“New data centers for artificial intelligence and the digital economy account for most of the projected increase in North American electricity demand over the next 10 years,” NERC’s report said.

The report indicated several parts of the country will be at “high risk” for electricity supply shortfalls by the end of the decade.

It said that states served by the Midcontinent Independent System Operator could be at high risk starting in 2028, while parts of Texas, the northwestern U.S., the mid-Atlantic and parts of the Midwest could be at high risk starting in 2029.

The report also warned that most new power set to come online includes solar and battery storage, which the report described as “inverter-based and weather-dependent resources that increase the complexity of planning and operating a reliable grid.”

It also noted that fossil fuel plants may retire in the next five years, “reducing the amount of generation that has fuel on site and impacting the system’s ability to respond to spikes in demand.”

NERC is a regulatory authority responsible for the continental U.S., Canada and the northern part of Baja California, Mexico. It was founded by representatives of the electric utility industry and was later designated by the U.S. government as the Electric Reliability Organization that develops and enforces standards for the grid.

“This assessment is not a prediction of failure but an early warning on the trajectory of risk,” said John Moura, NERC’s director of Reliability Assessment and Performance Analysis, in a statement. “The path forward is still manageable but only if planned resources come online and on time.”

To address the issues, the report recommends expediting new power sources and approval processes, among other strategies.

The Trump administration has broadly embraced data centers and has sought to fast-track their buildout — though recent messaging from the administration has also said they should bring their own power sources online.

The administration has been supportive of fossil fuels and has sought to hamper renewable energy projects.

data.title: Buttigieg endorses Klobuchar's Minnesota governor bid after they clashed in 2020 Dem primary
data.description:
Sen. Amy Klobuchar’s (D-Minn.) former Democratic rival, Pete Buttigieg, has backed her bid to be Minnesota’s next governor. 

The former Transportation secretary and former mayor of South Bend, Indiana, said he is “thrilled to support” Klobuchar following the senator’s campaign launch on Thursday.

“I’ve worked alongside her and seen firsthand how she shows up, listens, and takes action to get results for the people she serves,” Buttigieg posted on the social platform X on Thursday. “Minnesotans deserve steady, caring leadership now more than ever – and they will be very well served with Amy leading their state.”

Buttigieg’s endorsement is a notable change of tone from the 2020 presidential campaign trail, during which the Midwestern politicians notably clashed on several points. 

The acrimony between the pair culminated at the Democratic primary debate in February 2020 when Buttigieg slammed Klobuchar over forgetting the name of the Mexican president in an interview with Telemundo. The Minnesota legislator responded, “Are you trying to say that I’m dumb? Or are you mocking me here, Pete?”

Klobuchar needled the former mayor over his lack of experience in Washington, D.C., saying, “I am the one, not you, that has won statewide, in congressional district after congressional district.”

Buttigieg responded by questioning Klobuchar for her voting record in Congress, including her decision to approve the nomination of former Customs and Border Protection acting chief, Kevin McAleenan, who was tasked with carrying out the first Trump administration’s hardline immigration agenda. 

“If you’re going to run on your record in Washington, then you’ve got to own those votes, especially when it comes to immigration,” Buttigieg said. 

At the debate’s conclusion, Klobuchar rebuffed a customary handshake from him, signaling a split between the two moderate Democrats. 

On Thursday, Buttigieg seemed to put the bad blood behind him, calling Klobuchar, “my friend,” in his post endorsing her run.

If elected, Klobuchar would be Minnesota’s first female governor. The state’s primary will be held on Aug. 11.

data.title: Shapiro suggests 'strong case' to be made in Minnesota for voluntary manslaughter, obstruction
data.description:
Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) said Thursday there is a “very strong case” to charge the Customs and Border Protection (CBP) official who shot Alex Pretti multiple times on Saturday with voluntary manslaughter. 

“I see a case that requires a state investigation and requires a state determination as to whether prosecution is warranted,” Shapiro said at an event hosted by the Christian Science Monitor. 

“I would argue, based on the publicly available evidence, that you could make a very strong case for voluntary manslaughter against the federal official who pulled the trigger multiple times.”

Video of the incident shows Pretti recording immigration officials before attempting to assist a woman whom one agent had pushed to the ground. At that point, he was shoved down himself and surrounded by officers, with one appearing to disarm him before another fired multiple rounds at the 37-year-old.

CBP, which oversees Border Patrol, notified Congress on Tuesday that two agents fired shots during the incident, according to the Associated Press.

Shapiro, who previously served as the Keystone State’s attorney general, also said there is a “very strong case” the Border Patrol agents committed obstruction of justice when they did not secure the crime scene.

“I think you might be able to make out a case for conspiracy, if there was a clear directive that that crime scene should be compromised, that you should not participate in an investigation,” the Pennsylvania governor added. “So I think all three of those charges could be warranted.”

Shapiro noted, though, that he is “not privy” to all the evidence that Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison (D) and local prosecutors have regarding the shooting. 

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) took over the investigation into the incident over the objections of Drew Evans, the superintendent of the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension (BCA), which oversees use-of-force incidents by officers in the state. 

In a suit brought by the BCA, a federal judge in Minnesota blocked DHS on Saturday from “destroying or altering” evidence from its probe. 

White House border czar Tom Homan, meanwhile, said at a Thursday press conference that the federal immigration enforcement operation in Minnesota has not been “perfect.”

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on and what we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book,” said Homan, who President Trump dispatched to the state to handle the operation earlier this week.

data.title: Live updates: Senate vote pushes government closer to shutdown; Noem mum at Trump Cabinet meeting
data.description:
President Trump assembled his Cabinet for a meeting Thursday, touting his administration’s achievements after a year in office. He made some news in saying he would announce his nominee for Federal Reserve chair next week.

But in a break from tradition, not every Cabinet member spoke. Among those sitting and listening was Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, who has drawn immense scrutiny after two deaths at the hands of federal immigration officials in recent weeks.

On Thursday afternoon, Trump is slated to sign an executive order to coordinate a federal plan to combat drug addiction and substance abuse.

Meanwhile, on Capitol Hill, Senate Democrats and a few Republicans took down a procedural vote that would have advanced a major government funding package. Here’s why seven Republicans voted agaianst it. If a deal isn’t made by 11:59 p.m. Friday, part of the government will shut down.

Trump administration border czar Tom Homan said at a press conference Thursday morning that the immigration crackdown in Minneapolis will be done “by the book” as Trump looks to “fix” it.

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on. What we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book,” Homan said Thursday.

Trump later Thursday will join first lady Melania Trump at the premiere of her documentary at the Kennedy Center.

Follow live below.

data.title: Shapiro says state has 'ratcheted up' preparation if federal immigration crackdown comes to Pennsylvania
data.description:
Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) said Thursday that his administration is “preparing” should the Commonwealth become the next target of President Trump’s immigration crackdown amid growing concerns about clashes between residents and federal law enforcement officers across the country.

“I’m not going to get into specifics of what we’re doing … But I can tell you that we have ratcheted up our preparation should this come, and that is going to involve not just law enforcement response but working closely with the community,” Shapiro said during an event hosted by The Christian Science Monitor in Washington, D.C.

“And so I want the community to know where I stand. I hear them, and I want them to know that we’re gonna be in partnership together to make sure that they are safe and protected in our country,” Shapiro added.

Shapiro declined to go into further detail, noting only that his government was “prepared on every level.” He did, however, acknowledge there may be some limitations to his ability to respond should Trump decide to move in.

“If the President of the United States seeks to impose his will and the federal will on the Commonwealth, there may be some things we can’t stop,” Shapiro said, adding that he had taken lessons from previous federal deployments in California, Illinois and Minnesota.

“We have the benefit of hindsight there, and we’ve been preparing, and I think we have some things that we believe would be deployed to keep our citizens safe,” he continued.

Shapiro, who is running for reelection and widely seen as a possible 2028 presidential contender, railed against the Trump administration’s handling of immigration enforcement operations in Minnesota.

Protests have erupted across the Twin Cities and elsewhere over the fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens, Alex Pretti and Renee Good, by federal law enforcement officers this month during Operation Metro Surge.

“At the end of the day, the job of any executive is to keep their citizens safe: mayor, governor, president. It’s one of the reasons why I’m so troubled by what this president is doing. I mean, he claims to be the law-and-order president. He is literally undermining the safety and security of the people of Minnesota through these actions,” Shapiro said.

Trump has called to de-escalate the situation in Minneapolis as the White House tries to contain the political fallout from Pretti’s death, pulling some federal agents out of the state this week. He has not indicated where they could be headed next.

data.title: Newsom team mocks Trump social media blitz: 'Deep breaths everyone'
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom’s (D) press office on Wednesday mocked President Trump after he fired off almost 60 social media posts in one hour, with the press office commenting, “Deep breaths, everyone.”

“Just to clarify: Walmart’s 303 stores in California are open. The AI robot is lying. Governor Newsom is not Pablo Escobar,” Newsom’s press office wrote on the social platform X.

“We cannot believe we have to say any of this out loud,” Newsom’s media team wrote. “We cannot believe this is real life. And we truly cannot believe this man has the nuclear codes. Deep breaths, everyone. Three more years.”

Of Trump’s posts that Newsom’s press office referred to, one post claimed Walmart was closing 85 percent of its stores because employees were paid a $22 minimum wage. Another post claimed featured an artificial intelligence (AI)-generated person claiming Newsom and “the Mexican cartel” operate a drug-money laundering scheme out of the governor’s office.

Another post related to California featured a video of Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services Administrator Mehmet Oz in Los Angeles County claiming there was a welfare fraud scandal in California “far worse” than a similar growing scandal in Minnesota.

On Wednesday, the press office wrote on X, “So…about last night.” The post featured a Google search for “what is sundowning,” which refers to a state of confusion and other symptoms affecting older adults typically in the afternoon and lasts through the evening, according to the Mayo Clinic.

Newsom further dismissed the president’s claims while speaking with at a Bloomberg Newsmakers event in San Francisco.

“But you see how that travels everywhere,” Newsom said in a video of the event shared on X by his media team. “It may even travel to one of your inboxes. Maybe it’s a tweet, a retweet. ‘I didn’t say it, it was said.’ All of a sudden it’s in a blog. All of a sudden there’s [Fox News host] Jesse Watters. All of a sudden there’s [Fox News host] Sean Hannity. All of a sudden we’re having a conversation where I have to say, ‘No, it’s not true. I’m not involved with the Mexican cartels.”

Newsom, an outspoken critic of the president and a likely contender for the Democratic nomination for president in 2028, frequently mocks Trump and has sometimes done it using AI.

These AI posts have varied from depicting Marie Antoinette with Trump’s face to showing Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller in handcuffs. The California Democrat has also written posts on X that mock Trump’s writing style on social media.

Trump announced on Tuesday that he signed an executive order to speed up the rebuilding of homes in areas impacted by the devastating Palisades fire. The order also directs the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) and the Small Business Administration to issue regulations that preempt state and local regulations to expedite the building process.

Newsom came to Washington, D.C., on Tuesday to advocate for the Golden State in receiving FEMA aid to help the communities still recovering from the Palisades fire.

“Donald Trump has signed a useless Executive Order rather than deliver the $34 BILLION California’s tax payers DESERVE in wildfire disaster aid to actually rebuild!” Newsom wrote on X. “It’s been over a year and he has refused to help the American people. Disgusting.”

data.title: Trump approval rating drops to 37 percent: Pew poll
data.description:
President Trump’s approval rating is slipping, with more than one-third of Americans saying they disapprove of how the president is handling his job, according to a new Pew Research Center poll.

The poll, released Thursday, found that Trump’s approval rating fell 3 percent from last fall and now stands at 37 percent. Trump’s support among Republicans remains high at 73 percent approval, though that figure is down slightly from a poll conducted last September.

Just 25 percent of Republicans and 94 percent of Democrats said they disapprove of Trump’s job performance, while only 5 percent of Democrats expressed approval.

Half of Americans said the administration’s actions since taking office last year have been worse than they expected, compared to 21 percent who said better. About one-in-four people said the administration’s actions matched their expectations.

Fewer Americans, 27 percent, also said they support all or most of Trump’s policies, down from 35 percent when he returned to office last year, a trend researchers noted was largely driven by Republicans.

Many of those surveyed also expressed a lack of confidence in Trump on several metrics, including his leadership skills, mental fitness, and physical fitness.

Confidence in Trump’s respect for U.S. democratic values, ability to pick good advisers, and act ethically in office was also lower than last year.

A decline in Republican confidence contributed to an 8-percent drop in the overall confidence in Trump’s ethics compared to February 2025. More than half of Republicans, 55 percent, said then that they were extremely or very confident that Trump acted ethically at the start of his second term, compared to 42 percent now.

While overall confidence in Trump’s ethics in office was already relatively low last February, at 29 percent, it has declined 8 percentage points since.

Trump’s approval rating, especially among independent voters, has been waning in recent weeks as his administration faces widespread pushback over its handling of immigration enforcement.

The Pew poll was already in progress when a U.S. Border Patrol agent shot and killed 37-year-old Alex Pretti in Minneapolis on Saturday morning, the second fatal shooting involving federal law enforcement officers this month.

Democrats have criticized the administration for its response to the killings and its deportation tactics. Senators on Thursday blocked a bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) because it did not do enough rein in Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), inching the country closer to a partial government shutdown.

The poll found strong support among Democrats for their congressional leadership to push back against Trump, with 82 percent saying their leaders should stand up to the president even if it makes addressing “critical problems” more difficult.

The survey was conducted from Jan. 20-26, among 8,512 U.S. adults, and has a margin of error of 1.4 percentage points.

data.title: Gingrich: Time for 'national conversation' about undocumented migrants who 'obey the law'
data.description:
Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich (R-Ga.) on Wednesday said the country needs a national conversation about undocumented migrants who “obey the law,” as public opinion sours on the Trump administration’s deportation sweeps.

“We need a national conversation about what we’re going to do, about people who’ve come here, some of them 20 years ago, who’ve been obeying the law, paying taxes, good neighbors, have kids, go to PTA. Very few Americans want to see the police walk in and pick them up and deport them,” Gingrich said during an appearance on Fox News Business.

“On the other hand, people do not want to give them citizenship. So there should be some middle ground here on long-term goals,” he added.

Gingrich’s comments coincide with Fox News host Sean Hannity speaking out against workplace raids during immigration enforcement crackdowns nationwide.

“Now, is ICE perfect? They’re not. Can they do a better job? They can,” he said during Monday’s episode of “The Sean Hannity Show.” “Do I think that, you know, some of the optics at times, do I think that going into Home Depots and arresting people there is a good idea? I don’t.”

“I don’t think the people that — if you’re working at a place, you’re not the immediate problem. Eventually, we’ll have to deal with those people that didn’t respect our laws, borders and sovereignty,” he added.

Amid public outrage for the fatal shooting of two U.S. citizens at the hands of federal immigration enforcement officers, a growing number of Republicans and conservative commentators are urging the White House to shift course and scale back its aggressive immigration enforcement, especially for law-abiding immigrants with roots in their communities.

“There has got to be a program where people get to come forward,” Hannity said on Monday. “I think they should then be given transportation home, they can get a check for what, $2,000, and they can apply to come back in the country legally.”

“We can vet them, we can do a health check on them and make sure they won’t be a financial burden on the American people. I think that’d be a more responsible, reasonable way to deal with that problem,” he added.

MS Now’s Joe Scarborough struck a similar tone this week, suggesting “if you’ve been in America for a long time, if you’ve been law abiding, if you’re an asylum seeker, certainly if you’ve had children that have served in the military, you’re at the front of the line” to return to the United States if deported.”

data.title: Melania Trump documentary pulled from South African theaters
data.description:
First lady Melania Trump’s documentary is not making it to theaters in South Africa, according to a local distributor.

“Melania,” which opens Friday, outlines the run-up to her husband’s second term. The first lady was an executive producer on the film.

“Based on recent developments, we’ve taken the decision to not go ahead with a theatrical release in territory,” Filmfinity’s head of sales and marketing Thobashan Govindarajulu told The New York Times in a Wednesday report.

News24, a South African outlet, first reported on the pulling of the film.

In November, President Trump said that South Africa would not receive an invitation to this year’s Group of 20 (G20) Miami summit, ratcheting up tensions with South Africa due to allegations of mistreatment of white farmers.

The president put out the decision on Truth Social when the 2025 Johannesburg-based G20 was ending. Trump said the U.S. didn’t attend that summit “because the South African Government refuses to acknowledge or address the horrific Human Right Abuses endured by Afrikaners, and other descendants of Dutch, French, and German settlers.”

“To put it more bluntly, they are killing white people, and randomly allowing their farms to be taken from them,” Trump alleged.

South African officials have rejected the Trump administration’s claims, saying there is no evidence of genocide or persecution of Afrikaners in the country.

Kennedy Center interim President Richard Grenell said earlier this month that “Melania” will premiere there Thursday. Both the president and first lady are slated to attend.

Amazon reportedly invested $40 million in the film, which critics have questioned.

The Hill has reached out to Amazon MGM Studios, the White House and Filmfinity for comment.

data.title: Majority of Americans say Trump's rhetoric about China hurts Chinese-Americans: Survey
data.description:
A majority of Americans think that President Trump’s rhetoric and language when discussing China and relations with Beijing “negatively impacts” how people of Chinese descent are treated in the U.S., according to a new survey. 

Fifty-four percent of respondents said Trump’s rhetoric has a negative impact on people of Chinese descent, 41 percent reported neither a positive or negative impact and just five percent reporting a positive impact, the survey, a collaboration between the Committee of 100 and the National Opinion Research Center at the University of Chicago, found.

“U.S.-China relations deeply affect how Chinese Americans here are perceived,” said Sheryl Wudunn, a member of Committee of 100 and a Chinese-American reporter, said during a webinar last week. “We all saw what happened during COVID when COVID was referred to as Kung flu or China virus and what that did to Chinese Americans.” 

Trump said he backed off that rhetoric at the request of Chinese President Xi Jinping, during remarks to the World Economic Forum in Davos last week. 

“I used to call it the China virus, but he said, do you think you could use a different name and I decided to do that because why should we have a problem over that?” Trump said.

White House Deputy Press Secretary Anna Kelly, responding to the survey, touted the president’s China policies.

“What comments?” she said in an emailed statement to The Hill. “President Trump has maintained a positive relationship with President Xi, while simultaneously putting America First by protecting our national security, leveling the playing field for our farmers, and securing an agreement from China to crack down on fentanyl precursors.”

The percentage of Americans who have an unfavorable opinion of China has climbed significantly in the last 20 years, according to Pew Research Center. Despite perceptions improving slightly in the past two years, about eight in ten Americans still report an unfavorable view of China. 

Rhetoric from political leaders is among the factors driving these attitudes, the survey found. The language used in news coverage also made a difference. 

Researchers found that participants who read a fake news article that assigned blame for intellectual property theft to the “Chinese government” were more likely to view Chinese immigrants “very favorably” compared to those who read an article attributing the actions to “China.” They were also more likely to see Chinese Americans as “very loyal” to the U.S.

While the results are particular to China and people of Chinese descent, Research lead Sam Collitt said differentiating people versus government in language could impact the perceptions of those descended from other foreign adversaries like North Korea and Russia as well. 

“We saw that that kind of precision in language really does have a bearing on the kind of downstream effects that has on perceptions of people of Chinese descent,” Collitt said. “And given the fact that ‘China’ is what’s used in most news reporting and most politicians, messaging, policy matters, is not ‘Chinese government,’ it’s ‘China.’” 

The survey, released on Jan. 21, also found that such language may be impacting views of whether Chinese-Americans were more loyal to China than to the U.S. After reading an article using “Chinese government,” 21 percent of respondents said Chinese-Americans were “much more loyal to the U.S.,” compared to 11 percent when the article used “China.” 

These attitudes and language choices have a significant impact on Chinese-Americans, said ,” said Cindy Tsai, executive vice president and general counsel of Committee of 100.

“I do think that there is that direct impact between the belonging and the mental health and feeling secure in this country,” she said. 

Sixty percent of Americans also reported wanting the U.S. to work more closely with China on diplomatic issues and policies impacting both countries. This included 65 percent of Democrats, 63 percent of Republicans and 39 percent of independents. 

Collitt said the majorities in both parties indicate “pretty clear bipartisan support for a better working relationship,” during a webinar last week. 

“The fact that we find that people are making a direct connection between what relations look like between two countries, as well as specifically how that’s messaged, from party leaders, from the government, from media, and how that has a direct bearing on people’s lives, and so far as how they’re treated, as their sense of belonging, mental health, I think the fact that that through line is being drawn by the American public, at least indicated by our data, is pretty astounding,” Collitt said. “And I think it speaks to the kind of the severity of the effects that are taking place.”

The survey also asked respondents whether they supported a ban on students and scholars from China coming to the U.S. to collaborate on research after the Trump administration threatened to “aggressively” revoke visas for Chinese graduate students affiliated with the Chinese Communist Party or studying in “critical fields.” 

Multiple Republican lawmakers have also proposed blocking federal funding to researchers with any ties to China citing concerns that the country could be exploiting open research for military gains.

Despite the administration citing national security threats, only 23 percent of Americans support prohibiting Chinese students and researchers from coming to the U.S. to do research, according to the survey. This includes just 16 percent of Democrats, 34 percent of Republicans and 18 percent of independents. 

“As we try to make sure the United States remains the leader in terms of scientific research we need to make sure we’re attracting the people who are the leading scientific minds in the world and these will include Chinese,” Madelina Hsu, a professor of history at the University of Maryland-College Park, said as a panelist during the webinar. 

“To try to label on the basis of race or national origin who may or may not be spies is a crude instrument. It’s not going to improve our national security.”
